titleStmt.title: The American Minister to the Secretary of State..

div.head: The American Minister to the Secretary of State .
div.p: Sir: I have the honor to inform the Department that Rear Admiral W. H. H. Southerland, U. S. N., Commander-in-Chief of the United States Pacific Fleet, sailed from Corinto, Nicaragua, on November 14, 1912, for San Diego, aboard his flagship the U. S. S California, accompanied by the other vessels of the fleet.

At his request I sent an informal letter inviting President Díaz and the Cabinet to visit the California the day before sailing. This invitation was accepted, and very interesting ceremonies were observed, including full presidential honors to the chief guest. Speeches were made by the Admiral, the President and others, expressive of great friendliness and cordiality, and the whole affair passed off successfully without any untoward incident. An account of the visit was given in the Gaceta Oficial of November 13 and 14, copies of which have been sent to the Division of Information.

Colonel J. H. Pendleton, U. S. M. C., in command of the First Provisional regiment, and Major Smedley D. Butler, U. S. M. C., in command of the battalion of marines from the Canal Zone, left together on the 22nd instant for Panama, thus withdrawing all the
United States forces from Nicaragua except one battalion at León under command of Lieutenant Colonel Charles G. Long, U. S. M. C., and one company at Managua under Captain Robert C. Underwood, U. S. M. C.
The deportment of all these forces during their stay in Nicaragua has been exemplary in every way and has been the subject of favorable comment from many different sources.

If it may be done without seeming invidious, the Legation desires to make special mention of the services rendered by Major Butler and his capable adjutant Lieutenant E. A. Catermann, and by Lieutenant Colonel Long, Lieutenant E. H. Conger and Corporal George Copeland, the two last mentioned of whom volunteered to carry Legation despatches under circumstances believed at the time to be extremely dangerous. Major Butler was the first commanding officer to arrive here, and by his energy, tact and determination succeeded in reestablishing order in Managua and Granada. Colonel Long accomplished the same difficult task in León, both of them demonstrating that a competent officer adds at least fifty per cent to the efficiency of his command for work such as has been recently done in this country.

The Legation availed of the opportunity to express its thanks in person to Admiral Southerland and Colonel Pendleton for the services rendered by them, their officers and men during the time they were engaged in protecting American life and property in Nicaragua.
